architecture.summary: A frozen medical vision-language model (VLM) is steered at decoding time via sparse autoencoder (SAE) residual editing. Per-token hidden states from late transformer layers are encoded into sparse representations by layer-wise Top-K SAEs. Clinical error types (false findings, missing findings, wrong location, wrong severity) are linked to specific SAE features via causal screening: features are ablated on a validation set and ranked by their measured effect on GREEN error counts. At inference, identified suppress features are zeroed and boost features are scaled; the resulting residual delta is added back to the frozen hidden state at four intervention layers. This preserves the original activations while injecting only the intentional edit, avoiding SAE reconstruction error.
trained_components: causal feature selection ranks (per error type)

biblio.title: Universal Boosts, Specific Suppressors: Sparse Autoencoder Steering of Medical Vision-Language Models

claims.metric: GREEN score; RadGraph F1-simple; Composite Score
claims.statement: On MIMIC-CXR test split, SAE residual steering improves RadVLM GREEN score by +1.1 percentage points absolute (+5.4% relative), RadGraph by +2.4 pp (+12.8% rel.), Composite by +1.7 pp (+5.4% rel.), with statistical significance at p<0.05 via paired bootstrap.
SAE steering generalizes to LLaVA-Rad (7B), improving GREEN by +2.9 pp (+10.2% rel.) and Composite by +2.4 pp (+7.2% rel.) on MIMIC-CXR test.

SAE steering on CheXOne (Qwen2.5-VL-3B, GRPO-tuned) improves GREEN by +2.6 pp (+11.7% rel.) and Composite by +4.2 pp (+17.0% rel.) on MIMIC-CXR test, despite the model already being fine-tuned with reinforcement learning.

Zero-shot transfer of RadVLM-trained SAE features and hyperparameters to IU-Xray (without retraining or re-tuning) achieves GREEN +3.8 pp (+7.7% rel.) and Composite +2.5 pp (+5.4% rel.), confirming features are model properties rather than corpus artifacts.

claims.dataset: MIMIC-CXR; RadVLM, LLaVA-Rad, CheXOne pairwise comparisons
claims.statement: Cross-model feature alignment shows boost features are highly consistent across architectures (weighted Jaccard 0.65–0.75, cosine 0.91–0.95 with 95% CI), whereas suppress features are model-specific (Jaccard 0.41–0.58, cosine 0.25–0.52). This means quality-promoting directions transfer, but hallucination-linked directions should be re-screened per backbone.

Hallucination reduction via SAE steering is not a free lunch but an error redistribution: steering recovers missing findings (MF –654 errors) at the cost of adding false findings (FF +1,112 errors), wrong location (+168), and wrong severity (+72). GREEN still improves because its structure asymmetrically rewards MF recovery (adds to numerator and subtracts denominator) relative to new FF/WL/WS errors (only enlarge denominator).

The paper's absolute GREEN improvements are moderate (1.1–3.8 pp) compared with task-specific fine-tuning or RL, and the authors do not present SAE steering as a replacement for those methods. The main contribution is interpretability and mechanistic introspection, not metric dominance.

Causal feature screening (single-feature ablation on validation set) is chosen over correlation-based selection because it reveals per-error-type effects: the same SAE coordinate can boost one error type while suppressing another (e.g., feature #1925 is MF-boost and WL-suppress). Residual steering (Eq. 4) outperforms standard SAE-mediated activation patching (Eq. 3) because it cancels SAE reconstruction error and preserves unedited knowledge.
